content.content:
1	# How to Put Your Website Live at www.michaelquintin.com
2	
3	This guide covers everything: initial setup, hosting, and the automated publish tool that keeps your site and ontology up to date.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Part 1 — Initial Setup (Do This Once)
8	
9	### 1.1 Install Git on Your Mac
10	
11	Open **Terminal** (search for it in Spotlight) and run:
12	
13	```bash
14	git --version
15	```
16	
17	If Git is installed, you'll see a version number. If not, macOS will prompt you to install it — click Install.
18	
19	### 1.2 Install Python Dependencies
20	
21	The publish tool uses Python (which comes pre-installed on macOS). Run:
22	
23	```bash
24	pip3 install anthropic PyPDF2 python-docx
25	```
26	
27	### 1.3 Get a Claude API Key
28	
29	1. Go to **https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys**
30	2. Create an account (or log in)
31	3. Click **Create Key**
32	4. Copy the key (it starts with `sk-ant-...`)
33	5. Open the file `tools/config.json` in your website folder and replace `YOUR_API_KEY_HERE` with your key:
34	
35	```json
36	{
37	  "anthropic_api_key": "sk-ant-your-actual-key-here",
38	  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
39	  "site_root": "..",
40	  "github_repo": "mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io",
41	  "auto_push": true
42	}
43	```
44	
45	**Important**: This file is listed in `.gitignore` so it will NEVER be uploaded to GitHub. Your API key stays on your computer only.
46	
47	### 1.4 Create the GitHub Repository
48	
49	1. Go to **https://github.com** and log in as **mrquintin**
50	2. Click **+** → **New repository**
51	3. Repository name: `mrquintin.github.io`
52	4. Make sure **Public** is selected
53	5. Leave everything else unchecked
54	6. Click **Create repository**
55	
56	### 1.5 Push Your Website to GitHub
57	
58	Open Terminal, navigate to your website folder, and run these commands:
59	
60	```bash
61	cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
62	git init
63	git add -A
64	git commit -m "Initial site upload"
65	git branch -M main
66	git remote add origin https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io.git
67	git push -u origin main
68	```
69	
70	Replace `~/path/to/Personal_Website` with wherever the folder is on your Mac.
71	
72	If GitHub asks for authentication, you'll need a **Personal Access Token**:
73	1. Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens → **Generate new token (classic)**
74	2. Give it a name like "website", check the **repo** scope
75	3. Click **Generate token** and copy it
76	4. Use this token as your password when Git prompts you
77	
78	### 1.6 Enable GitHub Pages
79	
80	1. Go to **https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io/settings/pages**
81	2. Under "Source", select **Deploy from a branch**
82	3. Under "Branch", select **main** / **/ (root)**
83	4. Click **Save**
84	
85	Within 2 minutes your site will be live at `https://mrquintin.github.io`.
86	
87	### 1.7 Connect Your Custom Domain
88	
89	**On GitHub:**
90	1. Still on Settings → Pages
91	2. Under **Custom domain**, type: `www.michaelquintin.com`
92	3. Click **Save**
93	
94	**On your domain registrar** (wherever you bought the domain — GoDaddy, Namecheap, Cloudflare, etc.):
95	
96	Find "DNS Management" and add these records:
97	
98	| Type  | Name/Host | Value                    | TTL       |
99	|-------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
100	| CNAME | www       | mrquintin.github.io      | Automatic |
101	| A     | @         | 185.199.108.153          | Automatic |
102	| A     | @         | 185.199.109.153          | Automatic |
103	| A     | @         | 185.199.110.153          | Automatic |
104	| A     | @         | 185.199.111.153          | Automatic |
105	
106	The CNAME makes `www.michaelquintin.com` work. The four A records make `michaelquintin.com` (without www) work. These are GitHub's official IP addresses.
107	
108	After 15-60 minutes, go back to GitHub Pages settings and check **Enforce HTTPS**.
109	
110	---
111	
112	## Part 2 — Publishing New Work (The Automated Workflow)
113	
114	This is the main thing you'll do going forward. Whenever you create something new — a paper, a project, an essay, a game, anything — you run one command and the tool:
115	
116	1. Reads your project files (PDF, code, markdown, Word docs, etc.)
117	2. Sends the content to Claude, which analyzes it
118	3. Claude generates a portfolio card AND figures out what concepts your work explores
119	4. Those concepts get added to your ontology graph with connections to your existing ideas
120	5. Everything gets committed and pushed to GitHub
121	6. Your live site updates in about a minute
122	
123	### How to Publish
124	
125	Open Terminal, navigate to your website folder, and run:
126	
127	```bash
128	cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
129	python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/your/project
130	```
131	
132	That's it. The tool handles everything else.
133	
134	### Examples
135	
136	**Publish a research paper (PDF):**
137	```bash
138	python3 tools/publish.py ~/Documents/distributed-systems-paper.pdf
139	```
140	
141	**Publish a code project (folder):**
142	```bash
143	python3 tools/publish.py ~/Code/my-vr-game/
144	```
145	
146	**Publish an essay (markdown):**
147	```bash
148	python3 tools/publish.py ~/Writing/ai-ethics-essay.md
149	```
150	
151	**Publish with a custom title and type:**
152	```bash
153	python3 tools/publish.py ~/Design/brand-identity/ --title "Brand Identity System" --type Design
154	```
155	
156	**Preview what Claude would generate (without changing anything):**
157	```bash
158	python3 tools/publish.py ~/Documents/paper.pdf --dry-run
159	```
160	
161	**Update files locally without pushing to GitHub:**
162	```bash
163	python3 tools/publish.py ~/Code/project/ --no-push
164	```
165	
166	### What Happens Step by Step
167	
168	When you run the command:
169	
170	1. The tool reads your files and shows a summary
171	2. It sends the content to Claude for analysis
172	3. Claude responds with:
173	   - A **project card** (title, type, description, tags)
174	   - **New concept nodes** for the ontology (the ideas in your work)
175	   - **New connections** between existing concepts that your work reveals
176	4. You see a preview of all changes and are asked to confirm
177	5. On confirmation, the tool:
178	   - Copies your project files into `projects/your-project-name/`
179	   - Adds the project card to `js/site.js`
180	   - Adds the ontology nodes and connections to `js/ontology-data.js`
181	   - Commits and pushes to GitHub
182	6. Your site updates live in ~1 minute
183	
184	### Options
185	
186	| Flag | What it does |
187	|------|-------------|
188	| `--title "Name"` | Override the project title (otherwise Claude picks one) |
189	| `--type Software` | Override the category: Software, Research, Creative, Design, Writing |
190	| `--dry-run` | Preview Claude's analysis without changing any files |
191	| `--no-push` | Update local files but don't push to GitHub |
192	| `--no-copy` | Don't copy project files to the site (just update the data) |
193	
194	---
195	
196	## Part 3 — What Each File Does
197	
198	| File | Purpose |
199	|------|---------|
200	| `index.html` | Main site — all sections (hero, about, projects, resume, blog, contact) |
201	| `js/site.js` | **Project cards, resume, blog data** — the publish tool auto-updates this |
202	| `js/ontology-data.js` | **Concept graph data** — the publish tool auto-updates this |
203	| `css/style.css` | All styling and theme variables |
204	| `pages/ontology.html` | The interactive ontology graph page |
205	| `pages/project-detail.html` | Template for detailed project write-ups |
206	| `blog/post-template.html` | Template for blog articles |
207	| `projects/` | Folder for project files (the tool copies files here automatically) |
208	| `tools/publish.py` | The publish CLI tool |
209	| `tools/config.json` | Your API key and settings (never uploaded to GitHub) |
210	| `CNAME` | Tells GitHub your custom domain |
211	| `.gitignore` | Protects config.json from being pushed |
212	
213	---
214	
215	## Part 4 — Manual Editing
216	
217	You can also edit anything by hand if you prefer.
218	
219	**Edit the ontology manually:**
220	Open `js/ontology-data.js` and add a node to the `ONTOLOGY_NODES` array:
221	```javascript
222	{
223	  id: "my-concept",
224	  label: "My Concept",
225	  category: "philosophy",
226	  description: "What this concept means.",
227	  size: "md",
228	  links: ["emergence", "complexity"]
229	}
230	```
231	
232	**Edit a project card manually:**
233	Open `js/site.js` and add to the `projects` array:
234	```javascript
235	{
236	  title: "Project Name",
237	  type: "Software",
238	  description: "What it does.",
239	  tags: ["React", "AI"],
240	  link: "#",
241	  date: "2026"
242	}
243	```
244	
245	**Push manual changes:**
246	```bash
247	cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
248	git add -A
249	git commit -m "Manual update"
250	git push
251	```
252	
253	---
254	
255	## Troubleshooting
256	
257	**"pip3: command not found":**
258	Run `python3 -m pip install anthropic PyPDF2 python-docx` instead.
259	
260	**"git: command not found":**
261	Install Xcode command line tools: `xcode-select --install`
262	
263	**publish.py says "API key not set":**
264	Edit `tools/config.json` and paste your Anthropic API key.
265	
266	**Site shows 404 after pushing:**
267	Check that `index.html` is at the root level of the repository (not inside a subfolder).
268	
269	**Ontology page is blank:**
270	Check the browser console (right-click → Inspect → Console) for JavaScript errors. Usually means a syntax issue in `ontology-data.js`.
271	
272	**DNS not working:**
273	Wait up to 48 hours. Flush DNS cache on Mac: `sudo dscacheutil -flushcache`
274	
275	**Git asks for password every time:**
276	Cache your credentials: `git config --global credential.helper osxkeychain`
277	
278	---
279	
280	## Quick Reference
281	
282	| What | Command / URL |
283	|------|--------------|
284	| Publish new work | `python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/project` |
285	| Preview without changes | `python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/project --dry-run` |
286	| Push manual edits | `git add -A && git commit -m "update" && git push` |
287	| Your live site | https://www.michaelquintin.com |
288	| Ontology page | https://www.michaelquintin.com/pages/ontology.html |
289	| GitHub repo | https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io |
290	| GitHub Pages settings | https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io/settings/pages |
291	| API key management | https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys |
292

file.content:
# How to Put Your Website Live at www.michaelquintin.com

This guide covers everything: initial setup, hosting, and the automated publish tool that keeps your site and ontology up to date.

---

## Part 1 — Initial Setup (Do This Once)

### 1.1 Install Git on Your Mac

Open **Terminal** (search for it in Spotlight) and run:

```bash
git --version
```

If Git is installed, you'll see a version number. If not, macOS will prompt you to install it — click Install.

### 1.2 Install Python Dependencies

The publish tool uses Python (which comes pre-installed on macOS). Run:

```bash
pip3 install anthropic PyPDF2 python-docx
```

### 1.3 Get a Claude API Key

1. Go to **https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys**
2. Create an account (or log in)
3. Click **Create Key**
4. Copy the key (it starts with `sk-ant-...`)
5. Open the file `tools/config.json` in your website folder and replace `YOUR_API_KEY_HERE` with your key:

```json
{
  "anthropic_api_key": "sk-ant-your-actual-key-here",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  "site_root": "..",
  "github_repo": "mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io",
  "auto_push": true
}
```

**Important**: This file is listed in `.gitignore` so it will NEVER be uploaded to GitHub. Your API key stays on your computer only.

### 1.4 Create the GitHub Repository

1. Go to **https://github.com** and log in as **mrquintin**
2. Click **+** → **New repository**
3. Repository name: `mrquintin.github.io`
4. Make sure **Public** is selected
5. Leave everything else unchecked
6. Click **Create repository**

### 1.5 Push Your Website to GitHub

Open Terminal, navigate to your website folder, and run these commands:

```bash
cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
git init
git add -A
git commit -m "Initial site upload"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io.git
git push -u origin main
```

Replace `~/path/to/Personal_Website` with wherever the folder is on your Mac.

If GitHub asks for authentication, you'll need a **Personal Access Token**:
1. Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens → **Generate new token (classic)**
2. Give it a name like "website", check the **repo** scope
3. Click **Generate token** and copy it
4. Use this token as your password when Git prompts you

### 1.6 Enable GitHub Pages

1. Go to **https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io/settings/pages**
2. Under "Source", select **Deploy from a branch**
3. Under "Branch", select **main** / **/ (root)**
4. Click **Save**

Within 2 minutes your site will be live at `https://mrquintin.github.io`.

### 1.7 Connect Your Custom Domain

**On GitHub:**
1. Still on Settings → Pages
2. Under **Custom domain**, type: `www.michaelquintin.com`
3. Click **Save**

**On your domain registrar** (wherever you bought the domain — GoDaddy, Namecheap, Cloudflare, etc.):

Find "DNS Management" and add these records:

| Type  | Name/Host | Value                    | TTL       |
|-------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| CNAME | www       | mrquintin.github.io      | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.108.153          | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.109.153          | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.110.153          | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.111.153          | Automatic |

The CNAME makes `www.michaelquintin.com` work. The four A records make `michaelquintin.com` (without www) work. These are GitHub's official IP addresses.

After 15-60 minutes, go back to GitHub Pages settings and check **Enforce HTTPS**.

---

## Part 2 — Publishing New Work (The Automated Workflow)

This is the main thing you'll do going forward. Whenever you create something new — a paper, a project, an essay, a game, anything — you run one command and the tool:

1. Reads your project files (PDF, code, markdown, Word docs, etc.)
2. Sends the content to Claude, which analyzes it
3. Claude generates a portfolio card AND figures out what concepts your work explores
4. Those concepts get added to your ontology graph with connections to your existing ideas
5. Everything gets committed and pushed to GitHub
6. Your live site updates in about a minute

### How to Publish

Open Terminal, navigate to your website folder, and run:

```bash
cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/your/project
```

That's it. The tool handles everything else.

### Examples

**Publish a research paper (PDF):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Documents/distributed-systems-paper.pdf
```

**Publish a code project (folder):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Code/my-vr-game/
```

**Publish an essay (markdown):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Writing/ai-ethics-essay.md
```

**Publish with a custom title and type:**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Design/brand-identity/ --title "Brand Identity System" --type Design
```

**Preview what Claude would generate (without changing anything):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Documents/paper.pdf --dry-run
```

**Update files locally without pushing to GitHub:**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Code/project/ --no-push
```

### What Happens Step by Step

When you run the command:

1. The tool reads your files and shows a summary
2. It sends the content to Claude for analysis
3. Claude responds with:
   - A **project card** (title, type, description, tags)
   - **New concept nodes** for the ontology (the ideas in your work)
   - **New connections** between existing concepts that your work reveals
4. You see a preview of all changes and are asked to confirm
5. On confirmation, the tool:
   - Copies your project files into `projects/your-project-name/`
   - Adds the project card to `js/site.js`
   - Adds the ontology nodes and connections to `js/ontology-data.js`
   - Commits and pushes to GitHub
6. Your site updates live in ~1 minute

### Options

| Flag | What it does |
|------|-------------|
| `--title "Name"` | Override the project title (otherwise Claude picks one) |
| `--type Software` | Override the category: Software, Research, Creative, Design, Writing |
| `--dry-run` | Preview Claude's analysis without changing any files |
| `--no-push` | Update local files but don't push to GitHub |
| `--no-copy` | Don't copy project files to the site (just update the data) |

---

## Part 3 — What Each File Does

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `index.html` | Main site — all sections (hero, about, projects, resume, blog, contact) |
| `js/site.js` | **Project cards, resume, blog data** — the publish tool auto-updates this |
| `js/ontology-data.js` | **Concept graph data** — the publish tool auto-updates this |
| `css/style.css` | All styling and theme variables |
| `pages/ontology.html` | The interactive ontology graph page |
| `pages/project-detail.html` | Template for detailed project write-ups |
| `blog/post-template.html` | Template for blog articles |
| `projects/` | Folder for project files (the tool copies files here automatically) |
| `tools/publish.py` | The publish CLI tool |
| `tools/config.json` | Your API key and settings (never uploaded to GitHub) |
| `CNAME` | Tells GitHub your custom domain |
| `.gitignore` | Protects config.json from being pushed |

---

## Part 4 — Manual Editing

You can also edit anything by hand if you prefer.

**Edit the ontology manually:**
Open `js/ontology-data.js` and add a node to the `ONTOLOGY_NODES` array:
```javascript
{
  id: "my-concept",
  label: "My Concept",
  category: "philosophy",
  description: "What this concept means.",
  size: "md",
  links: ["emergence", "complexity"]
}
```

**Edit a project card manually:**
Open `js/site.js` and add to the `projects` array:
```javascript
{
  title: "Project Name",
  type: "Software",
  description: "What it does.",
  tags: ["React", "AI"],
  link: "#",
  date: "2026"
}
```

**Push manual changes:**
```bash
cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
git add -A
git commit -m "Manual update"
git push
```

---

## Troubleshooting

**"pip3: command not found":**
Run `python3 -m pip install anthropic PyPDF2 python-docx` instead.

**"git: command not found":**
Install Xcode command line tools: `xcode-select --install`

**publish.py says "API key not set":**
Edit `tools/config.json` and paste your Anthropic API key.

**Site shows 404 after pushing:**
Check that `index.html` is at the root level of the repository (not inside a subfolder).

**Ontology page is blank:**
Check the browser console (right-click → Inspect → Console) for JavaScript errors. Usually means a syntax issue in `ontology-data.js`.

**DNS not working:**
Wait up to 48 hours. Flush DNS cache on Mac: `sudo dscacheutil -flushcache`

**Git asks for password every time:**
Cache your credentials: `git config --global credential.helper osxkeychain`

---

## Quick Reference

| What | Command / URL |
|------|--------------|
| Publish new work | `python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/project` |
| Preview without changes | `python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/project --dry-run` |
| Push manual edits | `git add -A && git commit -m "update" && git push` |
| Your live site | https://www.michaelquintin.com |
| Ontology page | https://www.michaelquintin.com/pages/ontology.html |
| GitHub repo | https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io |
| GitHub Pages settings | https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io/settings/pages |
| API key management | https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys |

input.content:
# v2 Deployment Runbook

This runbook is the source of truth for deploying the v2 site from a clean
checkout. Follow it top-to-bottom for first-time setup, then refer to the
later sections for ongoing maintenance.

## Prerequisites

- **Vercel account** with the CLI installed (`npm i -g vercel`).
- **GitHub repository** connected to a Vercel project (the project's
  Production branch should track `main`).
- **Anthropic Console account** with an API key
  (`https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys`). v2 uses
  `claude-opus-4-7` by default.
- **Embedding provider account** — pick one:
  - **Voyage AI** (default, recommended): API key from
    `https://dash.voyageai.com/api-keys`, used with `EMBEDDER=voyage`.
  - **OpenAI** (alternative): API key from
    `https://platform.openai.com/api-keys`, used with `EMBEDDER=openai`.
- **Local toolchain**: Node 20+, npm 10+, and `psql` (only needed for the
  optional corpus sanity check below).

## First-time setup

1. **Provision Postgres.** In the Vercel dashboard go to
   *Storage → Create Database → Postgres*, accept the defaults, and link
   the new database to the Vercel project. This auto-populates
   `POSTGRES_URL` and `POSTGRES_URL_NON_POOLING` for every deployment
   environment.

2. **Provision KV (Upstash Redis).** In the Vercel dashboard go to
   *Storage → Create Database → KV*, link it to the project. This
   auto-populates `KV_REST_API_URL` and `KV_REST_API_TOKEN` (plus the
   read-only equivalents).

3. **Add application environment variables.** In *Project Settings →
   Environment Variables* add:

   | Variable | Required | Notes |
   |---|---|---|
   | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | yes | Production + Preview + Development |
   | `VOYAGE_API_KEY` | yes (if `EMBEDDER=voyage`) | Production + Preview + Development |
   | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | yes (if `EMBEDDER=openai`) | Production + Preview + Development |
   | `EMBEDDER` | yes | `voyage` (default) or `openai` |
   | `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` | yes | Set to the production URL (e.g. `https://www.michaelquintin.com`); use the Vercel preview URL for Preview scope |
   | `LLM_MODEL` | optional | Defaults to `claude-opus-4-7` |
   | `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR` | optional | Defaults from `lib/env.ts` |
   | `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY` | optional | Defaults from `lib/env.ts` |

   Pick the appropriate scope (Production, Preview, Development) for each
   variable. Secrets (`*_API_KEY`) belong in all three; `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL`
   is per-environment.

4. **Pull env locally.** From the project root:

   ```bash
   vercel link
   vercel env pull .env.local
   ```

   This writes the linked Postgres, KV, and application variables into
   `.env.local` so the local dev server and CLI scripts can connect.

5. **Run database migrations.**

   ```bash
   npm run db:migrate
   ```

   Migrations are forward-only by default. Re-running is safe.

6. **Seed the corpus.**

   ```bash
   # 1. Drop initial files (.md / .pdf / .txt) into corpus/
   # 2. Edit corpus/manifest.json to register each file with id, path, tags
   # 3. Ingest:
   npm run ingest
   ```

   The ingest script chunks each file, embeds via the configured
   `EMBEDDER`, and writes rows to the `chunks` table.

7. **First deploy.** Either:

   ```bash
   vercel --prod
   ```

   or push to `main` and let the GitHub integration auto-deploy. Both
   paths run the build, the postbuild sitemap step, and the PDF resume
   build.

## Subsequent deploys

- Merge to `main` → Vercel auto-builds and deploys to Production.
- Pull requests get Preview deployments automatically; they share the
  Production Postgres and KV unless overridden in the env scope.
- The sitemap is regenerated by the `postbuild` script on every build.
- The resume PDF is regenerated by the `build-pdf` script (see 16/P08).

## Updating the corpus

1. Add or edit files under `corpus/`.
2. Update `corpus/manifest.json` with new entries (or remove stale ones).
3. Re-ingest:

   ```bash
   npm run ingest
   ```

   Pass `--rebuild` if you want to wipe and reload all chunks rather than
   incrementally updating.

4. Confirm chunks landed in Postgres:

   ```bash
   psql $POSTGRES_URL -c "select count(*) from chunks;"
   ```

5. (Optional) Spot-check voice and groundedness:

   ```bash
   npm run evals:llm
   ```

## Cost expectations

- **Anthropic API.** v2 ships with `claude-opus-4-7`, which is the
  highest-tier model. A typical chat message ranges roughly
  **\$0.02–0.10** depending on context size and answer length. The
  per-IP and per-day rate limits configured via `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR`
  and `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY` keep the daily ceiling bounded — the worst
  case is roughly `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY × max-cost-per-message`.
- **Voyage embeddings.** ~\$0.0001 per 1K tokens. A v2-scale corpus
  (~500K tokens) costs **<\$0.10** to ingest from scratch. Re-ingest
  cost scales linearly with token churn.
- **Vercel Postgres.** The free tier comfortably fits the v2 corpus and
  expected query volume.
- **Vercel KV.** The free tier comfortably fits v2 traffic levels and
  the rate-limit counters.
- **Total ongoing.** Bounded by the rate limits above.
  **~\$5–20/month** is the expected band at low traffic; spikes are
  capped by the per-day LLM limit.

## Rollback

- **Code/runtime rollback.** Vercel keeps every prior deployment. From
  the project's *Deployments* tab, find the last known-good deployment
  and click *Promote to Production*. No CLI or git revert required.
- **Schema rollback.** DB migrations are forward-only by default. To
  unwind a schema change, write and apply a corresponding down migration
  manually against `$POSTGRES_URL`. There is no auto-down path.
- **Corpus rollback.** Corpus is in-repo; revert the offending commit
  and re-run `npm run ingest --rebuild`.

## Troubleshooting

- **"Unauthorized" on chat** → `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is missing or wrong
  for the deployment's environment scope. Re-check Vercel env vars and
  redeploy.
- **"No relevant material" on every query** → corpus is empty or
  ingestion silently failed. Re-run `npm run ingest --rebuild` and
  verify with the `psql` count above.
- **429s spiking** → confirm KV is connected (the rate limiter falls
  open if KV is unreachable, so spikes there usually mean it *is*
  connected and traffic is genuinely bursty). Raise
  `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR` / `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY` cautiously — every
  increase raises the daily cost ceiling.
- **PDF resume missing** → the `build-pdf` script failed during deploy.
  Check the Vercel build logs for Puppeteer/Playwright errors (missing
  Chromium, sandbox flags, font loading). Re-run locally with
  `npm run build-pdf` to reproduce.

tool_use_result.content:
# v2 Deployment Runbook

This runbook is the source of truth for deploying the v2 site from a clean
checkout. Follow it top-to-bottom for first-time setup, then refer to the
later sections for ongoing maintenance.

## Prerequisites

- **Vercel account** with the CLI installed (`npm i -g vercel`).
- **GitHub repository** connected to a Vercel project (the project's
  Production branch should track `main`).
- **Anthropic Console account** with an API key
  (`https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys`). v2 uses
  `claude-opus-4-7` by default.
- **Embedding provider account** — pick one:
  - **Voyage AI** (default, recommended): API key from
    `https://dash.voyageai.com/api-keys`, used with `EMBEDDER=voyage`.
  - **OpenAI** (alternative): API key from
    `https://platform.openai.com/api-keys`, used with `EMBEDDER=openai`.
- **Local toolchain**: Node 20+, npm 10+, and `psql` (only needed for the
  optional corpus sanity check below).

## First-time setup

1. **Provision Postgres.** In the Vercel dashboard go to
   *Storage → Create Database → Postgres*, accept the defaults, and link
   the new database to the Vercel project. This auto-populates
   `POSTGRES_URL` and `POSTGRES_URL_NON_POOLING` for every deployment
   environment.

2. **Provision KV (Upstash Redis).** In the Vercel dashboard go to
   *Storage → Create Database → KV*, link it to the project. This
   auto-populates `KV_REST_API_URL` and `KV_REST_API_TOKEN` (plus the
   read-only equivalents).

3. **Add application environment variables.** In *Project Settings →
   Environment Variables* add:

   | Variable | Required | Notes |
   |---|---|---|
   | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | yes | Production + Preview + Development |
   | `VOYAGE_API_KEY` | yes (if `EMBEDDER=voyage`) | Production + Preview + Development |
   | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | yes (if `EMBEDDER=openai`) | Production + Preview + Development |
   | `EMBEDDER` | yes | `voyage` (default) or `openai` |
   | `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` | yes | Set to the production URL (e.g. `https://www.michaelquintin.com`); use the Vercel preview URL for Preview scope |
   | `LLM_MODEL` | optional | Defaults to `claude-opus-4-7` |
   | `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR` | optional | Defaults from `lib/env.ts` |
   | `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY` | optional | Defaults from `lib/env.ts` |

   Pick the appropriate scope (Production, Preview, Development) for each
   variable. Secrets (`*_API_KEY`) belong in all three; `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL`
   is per-environment.

4. **Pull env locally.** From the project root:

   ```bash
   vercel link
   vercel env pull .env.local
   ```

   This writes the linked Postgres, KV, and application variables into
   `.env.local` so the local dev server and CLI scripts can connect.

5. **Run database migrations.**

   ```bash
   npm run db:migrate
   ```

   Migrations are forward-only by default. Re-running is safe.

6. **Seed the corpus.**

   ```bash
   # 1. Drop initial files (.md / .pdf / .txt) into corpus/
   # 2. Edit corpus/manifest.json to register each file with id, path, tags
   # 3. Ingest:
   npm run ingest
   ```

   The ingest script chunks each file, embeds via the configured
   `EMBEDDER`, and writes rows to the `chunks` table.

7. **First deploy.** Either:

   ```bash
   vercel --prod
   ```

   or push to `main` and let the GitHub integration auto-deploy. Both
   paths run the build, the postbuild sitemap step, and the PDF resume
   build.

## Subsequent deploys

- Merge to `main` → Vercel auto-builds and deploys to Production.
- Pull requests get Preview deployments automatically; they share the
  Production Postgres and KV unless overridden in the env scope.
- The sitemap is regenerated by the `postbuild` script on every build.
- The resume PDF is regenerated by the `build-pdf` script (see 16/P08).

## Updating the corpus

1. Add or edit files under `corpus/`.
2. Update `corpus/manifest.json` with new entries (or remove stale ones).
3. Re-ingest:

   ```bash
   npm run ingest
   ```

   Pass `--rebuild` if you want to wipe and reload all chunks rather than
   incrementally updating.

4. Confirm chunks landed in Postgres:

   ```bash
   psql $POSTGRES_URL -c "select count(*) from chunks;"
   ```

5. (Optional) Spot-check voice and groundedness:

   ```bash
   npm run evals:llm
   ```

## Cost expectations

- **Anthropic API.** v2 ships with `claude-opus-4-7`, which is the
  highest-tier model. A typical chat message ranges roughly
  **\$0.02–0.10** depending on context size and answer length. The
  per-IP and per-day rate limits configured via `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR`
  and `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY` keep the daily ceiling bounded — the worst
  case is roughly `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY × max-cost-per-message`.
- **Voyage embeddings.** ~\$0.0001 per 1K tokens. A v2-scale corpus
  (~500K tokens) costs **<\$0.10** to ingest from scratch. Re-ingest
  cost scales linearly with token churn.
- **Vercel Postgres.** The free tier comfortably fits the v2 corpus and
  expected query volume.
- **Vercel KV.** The free tier comfortably fits v2 traffic levels and
  the rate-limit counters.
- **Total ongoing.** Bounded by the rate limits above.
  **~\$5–20/month** is the expected band at low traffic; spikes are
  capped by the per-day LLM limit.

## Rollback

- **Code/runtime rollback.** Vercel keeps every prior deployment. From
  the project's *Deployments* tab, find the last known-good deployment
  and click *Promote to Production*. No CLI or git revert required.
- **Schema rollback.** DB migrations are forward-only by default. To
  unwind a schema change, write and apply a corresponding down migration
  manually against `$POSTGRES_URL`. There is no auto-down path.
- **Corpus rollback.** Corpus is in-repo; revert the offending commit
  and re-run `npm run ingest --rebuild`.

## Troubleshooting

- **"Unauthorized" on chat** → `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is missing or wrong
  for the deployment's environment scope. Re-check Vercel env vars and
  redeploy.
- **"No relevant material" on every query** → corpus is empty or
  ingestion silently failed. Re-run `npm run ingest --rebuild` and
  verify with the `psql` count above.
- **429s spiking** → confirm KV is connected (the rate limiter falls
  open if KV is unreachable, so spikes there usually mean it *is*
  connected and traffic is genuinely bursty). Raise
  `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR` / `LLM_RATE_LIMIT_DAY` cautiously — every
  increase raises the daily cost ceiling.
- **PDF resume missing** → the `build-pdf` script failed during deploy.
  Check the Vercel build logs for Puppeteer/Playwright errors (missing
  Chromium, sandbox flags, font loading). Re-run locally with
  `npm run build-pdf` to reproduce.

tool_use_result.originalFile:
# How to Put Your Website Live at www.michaelquintin.com

This guide covers everything: initial setup, hosting, and the automated publish tool that keeps your site and ontology up to date.

---

## Part 1 — Initial Setup (Do This Once)

### 1.1 Install Git on Your Mac

Open **Terminal** (search for it in Spotlight) and run:

```bash
git --version
```

If Git is installed, you'll see a version number. If not, macOS will prompt you to install it — click Install.

### 1.2 Install Python Dependencies

The publish tool uses Python (which comes pre-installed on macOS). Run:

```bash
pip3 install anthropic PyPDF2 python-docx
```

### 1.3 Get a Claude API Key

1. Go to **https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys**
2. Create an account (or log in)
3. Click **Create Key**
4. Copy the key (it starts with `sk-ant-...`)
5. Open the file `tools/config.json` in your website folder and replace `YOUR_API_KEY_HERE` with your key:

```json
{
  "anthropic_api_key": "sk-ant-your-actual-key-here",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  "site_root": "..",
  "github_repo": "mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io",
  "auto_push": true
}
```

**Important**: This file is listed in `.gitignore` so it will NEVER be uploaded to GitHub. Your API key stays on your computer only.

### 1.4 Create the GitHub Repository

1. Go to **https://github.com** and log in as **mrquintin**
2. Click **+** → **New repository**
3. Repository name: `mrquintin.github.io`
4. Make sure **Public** is selected
5. Leave everything else unchecked
6. Click **Create repository**

### 1.5 Push Your Website to GitHub

Open Terminal, navigate to your website folder, and run these commands:

```bash
cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
git init
git add -A
git commit -m "Initial site upload"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io.git
git push -u origin main
```

Replace `~/path/to/Personal_Website` with wherever the folder is on your Mac.

If GitHub asks for authentication, you'll need a **Personal Access Token**:
1. Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens → **Generate new token (classic)**
2. Give it a name like "website", check the **repo** scope
3. Click **Generate token** and copy it
4. Use this token as your password when Git prompts you

### 1.6 Enable GitHub Pages

1. Go to **https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io/settings/pages**
2. Under "Source", select **Deploy from a branch**
3. Under "Branch", select **main** / **/ (root)**
4. Click **Save**

Within 2 minutes your site will be live at `https://mrquintin.github.io`.

### 1.7 Connect Your Custom Domain

**On GitHub:**
1. Still on Settings → Pages
2. Under **Custom domain**, type: `www.michaelquintin.com`
3. Click **Save**

**On your domain registrar** (wherever you bought the domain — GoDaddy, Namecheap, Cloudflare, etc.):

Find "DNS Management" and add these records:

| Type  | Name/Host | Value                    | TTL       |
|-------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| CNAME | www       | mrquintin.github.io      | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.108.153          | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.109.153          | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.110.153          | Automatic |
| A     | @         | 185.199.111.153          | Automatic |

The CNAME makes `www.michaelquintin.com` work. The four A records make `michaelquintin.com` (without www) work. These are GitHub's official IP addresses.

After 15-60 minutes, go back to GitHub Pages settings and check **Enforce HTTPS**.

---

## Part 2 — Publishing New Work (The Automated Workflow)

This is the main thing you'll do going forward. Whenever you create something new — a paper, a project, an essay, a game, anything — you run one command and the tool:

1. Reads your project files (PDF, code, markdown, Word docs, etc.)
2. Sends the content to Claude, which analyzes it
3. Claude generates a portfolio card AND figures out what concepts your work explores
4. Those concepts get added to your ontology graph with connections to your existing ideas
5. Everything gets committed and pushed to GitHub
6. Your live site updates in about a minute

### How to Publish

Open Terminal, navigate to your website folder, and run:

```bash
cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/your/project
```

That's it. The tool handles everything else.

### Examples

**Publish a research paper (PDF):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Documents/distributed-systems-paper.pdf
```

**Publish a code project (folder):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Code/my-vr-game/
```

**Publish an essay (markdown):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Writing/ai-ethics-essay.md
```

**Publish with a custom title and type:**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Design/brand-identity/ --title "Brand Identity System" --type Design
```

**Preview what Claude would generate (without changing anything):**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Documents/paper.pdf --dry-run
```

**Update files locally without pushing to GitHub:**
```bash
python3 tools/publish.py ~/Code/project/ --no-push
```

### What Happens Step by Step

When you run the command:

1. The tool reads your files and shows a summary
2. It sends the content to Claude for analysis
3. Claude responds with:
   - A **project card** (title, type, description, tags)
   - **New concept nodes** for the ontology (the ideas in your work)
   - **New connections** between existing concepts that your work reveals
4. You see a preview of all changes and are asked to confirm
5. On confirmation, the tool:
   - Copies your project files into `projects/your-project-name/`
   - Adds the project card to `js/site.js`
   - Adds the ontology nodes and connections to `js/ontology-data.js`
   - Commits and pushes to GitHub
6. Your site updates live in ~1 minute

### Options

| Flag | What it does |
|------|-------------|
| `--title "Name"` | Override the project title (otherwise Claude picks one) |
| `--type Software` | Override the category: Software, Research, Creative, Design, Writing |
| `--dry-run` | Preview Claude's analysis without changing any files |
| `--no-push` | Update local files but don't push to GitHub |
| `--no-copy` | Don't copy project files to the site (just update the data) |

---

## Part 3 — What Each File Does

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `index.html` | Main site — all sections (hero, about, projects, resume, blog, contact) |
| `js/site.js` | **Project cards, resume, blog data** — the publish tool auto-updates this |
| `js/ontology-data.js` | **Concept graph data** — the publish tool auto-updates this |
| `css/style.css` | All styling and theme variables |
| `pages/ontology.html` | The interactive ontology graph page |
| `pages/project-detail.html` | Template for detailed project write-ups |
| `blog/post-template.html` | Template for blog articles |
| `projects/` | Folder for project files (the tool copies files here automatically) |
| `tools/publish.py` | The publish CLI tool |
| `tools/config.json` | Your API key and settings (never uploaded to GitHub) |
| `CNAME` | Tells GitHub your custom domain |
| `.gitignore` | Protects config.json from being pushed |

---

## Part 4 — Manual Editing

You can also edit anything by hand if you prefer.

**Edit the ontology manually:**
Open `js/ontology-data.js` and add a node to the `ONTOLOGY_NODES` array:
```javascript
{
  id: "my-concept",
  label: "My Concept",
  category: "philosophy",
  description: "What this concept means.",
  size: "md",
  links: ["emergence", "complexity"]
}
```

**Edit a project card manually:**
Open `js/site.js` and add to the `projects` array:
```javascript
{
  title: "Project Name",
  type: "Software",
  description: "What it does.",
  tags: ["React", "AI"],
  link: "#",
  date: "2026"
}
```

**Push manual changes:**
```bash
cd ~/path/to/Personal_Website
git add -A
git commit -m "Manual update"
git push
```

---

## Troubleshooting

**"pip3: command not found":**
Run `python3 -m pip install anthropic PyPDF2 python-docx` instead.

**"git: command not found":**
Install Xcode command line tools: `xcode-select --install`

**publish.py says "API key not set":**
Edit `tools/config.json` and paste your Anthropic API key.

**Site shows 404 after pushing:**
Check that `index.html` is at the root level of the repository (not inside a subfolder).

**Ontology page is blank:**
Check the browser console (right-click → Inspect → Console) for JavaScript errors. Usually means a syntax issue in `ontology-data.js`.

**DNS not working:**
Wait up to 48 hours. Flush DNS cache on Mac: `sudo dscacheutil -flushcache`

**Git asks for password every time:**
Cache your credentials: `git config --global credential.helper osxkeychain`

---

## Quick Reference

| What | Command / URL |
|------|--------------|
| Publish new work | `python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/project` |
| Preview without changes | `python3 tools/publish.py ~/path/to/project --dry-run` |
| Push manual edits | `git add -A && git commit -m "update" && git push` |
| Your live site | https://www.michaelquintin.com |
| Ontology page | https://www.michaelquintin.com/pages/ontology.html |
| GitHub repo | https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io |
| GitHub Pages settings | https://github.com/mrquintin/mrquintin.github.io/settings/pages |
| API key management | https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys |
